skill.name: research-grants
skill.description: Write competitive research proposals for NSF, NIH, DOE, DARPA, and Taiwan's NSTC when you need agency-compliant narratives, budgets, and review-criteria alignment for a specific solicitation/FOA/BAA.
skill.summary: Write competitive research proposals for NSF, NIH, DOE, DARPA, and Taiwan's NSTC with agency-compliant narratives, budgets, and review-criteria alignment.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 257 potential issues but all are false positives upon manual review. The skill is a legitimate grant-writing reference tool containing templates and agency guidelines. Weak cryptographic algorithm findings are false matches on academic reference text discussing research methods. Windows SAM database findings match the legitimate US government system name SAM.gov and the NSF program name LSAMP. Shell backtick patterns are markdown code block formatting, not executable commands. Hardcoded URLs are legitimate government agency and GitHub repository links appropriate for this skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Heuristic Dangerous Pattern (Dismissed - False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged a critical heuristic: dangerous combination of code execution, network access, and credential access. Manual review confirms this is a false positive. Code execution refers to markdown-formatted bash command examples in SKILL.md. Network access refers to legitimate government agency URLs in reference documents. Credential access was triggered by SAM.gov (System for Award Management, a US government system) being misidentified as Windows SAM database. The skill contains no executable code, no credential harvesting, and no malicious network calls.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm References (False Positives)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 50+ instances of 'weak cryptographic algorithm' across all files. These are false positives. The files are grant-writing templates and reference documents that discuss research methods, budget calculations, and academic content. Cryptographic terms appear in the context of describing research methodologies involving security or data integrity, not as executable cryptographic code being deployed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Windows SAM Database Detection (False Positives)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 'Windows SAM database' in 3 files. Manual review confirms these are false positives. In broader_impacts.md:271, 'SAM' appears in 'LSAMP' (Louis Stokes Alliances for Minority Participation, an NSF program). In doe_guidelines.md and references, 'SAM.gov' refers to the System for Award Management, a legitimate US government procurement system. Neither is related to Windows Security Account Manager.

low_findings.title: Shell Backtick Execution in Documentation (False Positives)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged inline code backticks as Ruby/shell command execution. These are markdown code block formatting characters used to display example commands, file paths, and reference names in documentation. No actual shell execution occurs within the skill's runtime context. Examples include 'python scripts/compliance_checker.py' and 'tlmgr install nstc-proposal' shown as usage examples for the human user.

low_findings.title: System and Network Reconnaissance References (False Positives)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged text fragments as reconnaissance attempts. These are references to research methodology, document navigation, and agency-specific terminology found in grant-writing guidelines. No actual reconnaissance scanning or system enumeration is performed.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Reference Documents (True Positive - Low Risk)
low_findings.description: The skill contains hardcoded URLs pointing to government agency websites, GitHub repositories, and package registries. All URLs are legitimate and expected for a grant-writing reference skill that links to agency guidelines (nsf.gov, grants.nih.gov, energy.gov, darpa.mil) and the open-source repository it belongs to. No malicious or exfiltration endpoints detected.

content.user_title: Write Competitive Research Grant Proposals for Federal Agencies
content.value_statement: Writing grant proposals for NSF, NIH, DOE, DARPA, and Taiwan NSTC requires strict agency compliance and strong alignment with review criteria. This skill provides templates, reference guides, and structured workflows to help you produce competitive, agency-compliant proposals that address specific solicitation requirements.
actual_capabilities: Draft agency-compliant proposal narratives for NSF, NIH, DOE, DARPA, and Taiwan NSTC.
Generate budget justifications that align personnel, equipment, and travel costs with the proposed workplan.
Structure NIH Specific Aims pages that address significance, innovation, and approach criteria.
Develop NSF Broader Impacts sections with specific, measurable, and integrated activities.
Create milestone timelines with go/no-go criteria and risk mitigation plans for phased projects.
Prepare resubmission strategies with structured responses to reviewer critiques.
limitations: Does not submit proposals to agency portals or manage institutional approval workflows.
Cannot guarantee funding outcomes because proposal success depends on many factors beyond content quality.
Reference materials may not include the latest agency policy changes; always verify current guidelines.
Budget calculations require manual verification; this skill provides templates, not certified financial tools.

use_cases.title: First-Time NIH Grant Application
use_cases.description: A new investigator needs to prepare an NIH R01 proposal. The skill guides them through Specific Aims page structure, Research Strategy sections, and review criteria alignment.
use_cases.target_user: Early-career researchers applying for their first major grant

use_cases.title: NSF Proposal with Strong Broader Impacts
use_cases.description: A faculty member needs to write an NSF proposal with competitive Broader Impacts. The skill provides templates and examples for integrating education, outreach, and societal benefit activities into the research plan.

use_cases.title: DARPA High-Risk High-Reward Proposal
use_cases.description: A research team responds to a DARPA BAA requiring phased execution plans, measurable milestones, and transition pathways. The skill helps structure the technical volume with go/no-go criteria and deliverables.
use_cases.target_user: Applied research groups pursuing DARPA funding

prompt_templates.title: Compare Funding Agencies
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the main differences between NSF and NIH research proposals in terms of review criteria, page limits, and budget requirements?
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner researcher comparing funding agencies for the first time

prompt_templates.title: Draft NIH Specific Aims
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me draft NIH R01 Specific Aims for a project studying [your topic]. Include a knowledge gap statement, central hypothesis, and three complementary aims with expected outcomes and potential pitfalls.
prompt_templates.scenario: Drafting the critical 1-page Specific Aims section for an NIH application

prompt_templates.title: Develop NSF Broader Impacts
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to write a Broader Impacts section for my NSF proposal on [your topic]. Suggest specific, measurable activities that integrate with the research plan and include evaluation methods.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developing the Broader Impacts section with equal weight to Intellectual Merit

prompt_templates.title: Review Budget Justification for Compliance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my budget justification for a [agency] proposal. Check that personnel effort levels match the workplan, equipment costs are justified by specific aims, and indirect costs comply with the institutional rate agreement.
prompt_templates.scenario: An experienced researcher validating budget-to-scope alignment before submission

output_examples.input: Help me understand what NIH reviewers look for in the Approach section
output_examples.output: NIH reviewers evaluate the Approach section based on five key criteria: clarity of the experimental design and how it matches each aim, adequacy of controls and sample sizes with statistical power, acknowledgement of potential pitfalls with alternative strategies, inclusion of quantitative rigor such as blinding and randomization, and a feasible timeline that matches the scope of work. The weakest areas are typically insufficient detail in methods and missing contingency plans for high-risk experiments.

output_examples.input: Draft an outline for an NSF Project Description on machine learning for climate modeling
output: 1. Introduction and Problem Statement (1-2 pages): Climate modeling gap between current resolution and computational cost, and why machine learning can bridge this gap.
2. Intellectual Merit (8-10 pages): Hybrid ML-physics model architecture, training data sources and preprocessing methods, validation against established climate model benchmarks.

3. Broader Impacts (3-4 pages): Undergraduate research training program integrated with the project, open-source software release with community workshops, and partnerships with NOAA and university climate research centers.

output_examples.input: Create milestones for a 3-year DOE research project on energy storage
output_examples.output: Year 1 Q1-Q2: Synthesis and characterization of new electrode materials. Year 1 Q3-Q4: Electrochemical testing and optimization with Go/No-Go decision at Month 12 (target: 80% capacity retention after 500 cycles). Year 2 Q1-Q2: Scale-up synthesis and prototype cell assembly. Year 2 Q3-Q4: Extended cycling and safety testing. Year 3 Q1-Q2: System integration and techno-economic analysis. Year 3 Q3-Q4: Final reporting and technology transfer documentation.

best_practices: Identify the specific solicitation or FOA number before drafting to ensure your proposal matches the exact agency requirements and format constraints.
Map every major claim in your narrative to a specific review criterion that reviewers will use to score your proposal.
Verify that personnel effort, equipment, travel, and other costs in your budget justification directly support the tasks described in your research plan.
anti_patterns: Treating Broader Impacts as an afterthought instead of integrating specific, measurable activities with evaluation plans into the research design.
Writing the same proposal content for different agencies without adapting to each agency's specific review criteria and mission priorities.
Promising research outcomes in the narrative without allocating corresponding resources and personnel effort in the budget justification.

faq.question: Which funding agencies does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports NSF, NIH, DOE, DARPA, and Taiwan NSTC proposals with agency-specific templates and review criteria.

faq.question: Does this skill submit proposals for me?
faq.answer: No. This skill helps you draft and structure proposal content. You must submit through the agency official portal.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-US funding agencies?
faq.answer: This skill is optimized for US agencies and Taiwan NSTC. It may not cover other international funding bodies.

faq.question: How current are the reference materials?
faq.answer: Reference materials reflect recent agency guidelines but may not include the latest policy changes. Always verify against official agency websites.

faq.question: Does this skill replace professional grant writers?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides templates and guidance but does not replace expert review by experienced grant professionals and your institutional research office.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for resubmissions?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes guidance on NIH A1 resubmission mechanics, including the Introduction to Resubmission structure and strategies for strengthening weak criteria.
